Taro Potato Leaf and Bellflowers
by Katsushika Hokusai

Basket of Peonies
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · 1810 or 1814 · color woodblock print; surimono
The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · 1814
A woman entwined with two octopuses — the larger embracing her, the smaller kissing her mouth. Hokusai's most notorious shunga print; one of the earliest explicit pieces to influence modern Western art.
Traveler Inscribing a Temple Pillar
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · c. 1830 or early 1830s · Woodblock print; ink and color on paper
South Wind, Clear Sky, from Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · early 1830s · color woodblock print
Rain Below the Mountain (from the series Thirty-six Views of Mt. Fuji)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · early 1830s · color woodblock print
Thirty-Six Views of Mt. Fuji: The Surface of Lake Misaka in Kai Province
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · early 1830s · color woodblock print
A View of Mount Fuji and Travellers by a Bridge
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · ca. 1835 · color woodblock print
The Brocade Bridge in Suo Province (from the series Curious Views of Famous Bridges in the Provinces)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · early 1830s · color woodblock print
The Great Wave off Kanagawa
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1831
A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.
Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1830–1832
Hokusai's other famous Fuji — a single triangular form lit crimson by morning sun, clouds streaming off the summit. From the same series as the Great Wave.
Fuji from Kanaya on the Tokaido Highway (Tōkaidō Kanaya no Fuji)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Japan · ca. 1831
The Minister Toru Daijin Standing by a Lake Beneath a Crescent Moon, from the series A True Mirror of Chinese and Japanese Poetry
by Katsushika Hokusai
